coredata.dc:title: Performance of all-solution-processed, durable 2D MoS2 flakes−BaTiO3 nanoparticles in polyvinylidene fluoride matrix nanogenerator devices using N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone polar solvent
coredata.dc:creator: Ng, Kong Eng
Ooi, Poh Choon
Shazni Mohammad Haniff, Muhammad Aniq
Goh, Boon Tong
Dee, Chang Fu
Chang, Wei Sea
Razip Wee, M.F. Mohd
Mohamed, Mohd Ambri
coredata.dc:description: Piezoelectric nanogenerator (PENG) is an alternative sustainable solution by transforming surrounding mechanical energy into electrical power. In this work, the optically transparent and mechanically flexible PENG devices were fabricated using simple all-solution-processed techniques. The device was constructed using spin- and spray coating methods to form the structure of silver nanowires/nanocomposite/poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate. The nanocomposite is a blending of barium titanite nanoparticles and molybdenum disulfide flakes in the polyvinylidene fluoride matrix. PENG devices were prepared with dimethylformamide (DMF) and N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvents, respectively to examine the influence of the solvents towards the output voltage. It is found that the PENG device prepared with the NMP solvent generated a voltage of 750 mV, significantly higher output voltage than the PENG device prepared by DMF, which is about 100 mV. The probable reasons could be the higher dipole moment of NMP solvent, interaction, and intercalation of MoS2 flakes in the PVDF polymer chain matrix. An automated self-tapping machine was designed to perform the endurance study. The device was able to generate stable 1.5 V of peak-to-peak voltage up to 5000 cycles in a total 5-day intermittent test without significant degradation. The PENG device prepared in the NMP solvent maintained at least 95% of its 1.5 V throughout the whole testing cycle.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide
Polyvinylidene fluoride
N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone
Nanogenerator
Piezoelectric

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported in-part by GUP-2018-082 from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia , Dana Modal Insan (Human Capital Fund) – MI-2019-005 and Dana Impak Perdana (Prime Impact Fund) – DIP-2018-006 from Research University Fund. This work was also supported in-part by University of Malaya (University Malaya Research Grant Programme (UMRG)) – AFR (Frontier Science) ( RP038C-17AFR ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the PENG device. The inset shows a picture of the fabricated PENG-NMP device, (b) SEM cross-sectional view of PENG-NMP device, (c) Side view of the schematic diagram for the self-assembled tapping design, and (d) Picture for the self-assembled tapping design.

Atomic structure of the MoS2–BaTiO3 nanocomposites. (a) TEM image of the BaTiO3 nanoparticles on the MoS2 flakes. The inset shows an HR-TEM image of the few-layer MoS2 at the edges on the selected green square area, (b) HR-TEM of the MoS2 flakes. The inset shows the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern with rotational fault stacking, (c) Fourier filtered HR-TEM image of the MoS2 flakes on the selected yellow square area in (b), (d) HR-TEM image of the BaTiO3 nanoparticles, the inset in (d) is a zoom-out TEM image displaying the cluster of the BaTiO3 nanoparticles, (e, f) SAED images of the few-layer MoS2 and the BaTiO3 nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Open circuit voltages measurements for PENG-NMP and PENG-DMF devices, (b) Open circuit voltages measurements for Reference-NMP and Reference-DMF devices, (c) Magnified view of electrical behaviour for PENG-NMP device, (d) Schematic working mechanism of the fabricated device.
XRD results of prominent peaks for β-phase PVDF dissolved in NMP and DMF solvents.
Transmittance test of bare PET, Reference-NMP, and PENG-NMP.
5-day endurance cycles test of the PENG-NMP device.

head.ce:title: Performance of all-solution-processed, durable 2D MoS2 flakes−BaTiO3 nanoparticles in polyvinylidene fluoride matrix nanogenerator devices using N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone polar solvent

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronic, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, Monash University Malaysia, Jalan Lagoon Selatan, Bandar Sunway, 47500, Subang Jaya, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, Monash University Malaysia, Jalan Lagoon Selatan, Bandar Sunway, 47500, Subang Jaya, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Piezoelectric nanogenerator (PENG) is an alternative sustainable solution by transforming surrounding mechanical energy into electrical power. In this work, the optically transparent and mechanically flexible PENG devices were fabricated using simple all-solution-processed techniques. The device was constructed using spin- and spray coating methods to form the structure of silver nanowires/nanocomposite/poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate. The nanocomposite is a blending of barium titanite nanoparticles and molybdenum disulfide flakes in the polyvinylidene fluoride matrix. PENG devices were prepared with dimethylformamide (DMF) and N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvents, respectively to examine the influence of the solvents towards the output voltage. It is found that the PENG device prepared with the NMP solvent generated a voltage of 750 mV, significantly higher output voltage than the PENG device prepared by DMF, which is about 100 mV. The probable reasons could be the higher dipole moment of NMP solvent, interaction, and intercalation of MoS2 flakes in the PVDF polymer chain matrix. An automated self-tapping machine was designed to perform the endurance study. The device was able to generate stable 1.5 V of peak-to-peak voltage up to 5000 cycles in a total 5-day intermittent test without significant degradation. The PENG device prepared in the NMP solvent maintained at least 95% of its 1.5 V throughout the whole testing cycle.

ce:section.ce:para: A piezoelectric generator is the harvest of surrounding mechanical energy and transforms it into electricity. Mechanical energies from surrounding and human activities are abundant, but most of them aren’t strong enough to be converted to useable energy source by conventional methods. Therefore, they will be ended up as unusable waste energy. Recently, with the development of nanogenerators and power-saving electronics, those type of weak mechanical energy has gained the interest of researchers and known as energy harvesting. It is one of the green alternatives to reduce our dependence on non-renewable chemical energy such as batteries and save our environment from pollution. It can be designed in tiny footage that is coined as a nanogenerator. Its miniature size, low cost, ease of fabrication makes it applicable to ease the installation, integration, and implementation in current existing circuits, devices, and systems. Piezoelectric nanogenerator (PENG) has been studied extensively over the past decades. As the term of nanogenerator suggests, nanomaterials such as nanowires, nanoparticles, and nanoflakes are involved in fabricating this device. The high surface to volume ratio, particularly the composite of nanomaterials tends to develop the certain properties of nanogenerator. Perceptibly, piezoelectric materials such as crystals, synthetic ceramics and polymers are studied widely by the researchers, developing these materials in nanoscale to enhance piezoelectricity [1].

Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is a piezoelectric polymer that is electrochemically stable, flexible and possessed outstanding mechanical strength, making it a suitable polymer binder for nanocomposite materials. Out of five crystalline phases, only the β and γ phase contributes to piezoelectricity due to the arrangement of strong electronegativity bond between C–F atoms, resulting in the dielectric moment that generating electric field [2]. The effort was made to determine the solubility of PVDF with 50 distinctive solvents. Among the liquids, dimethylacetamide (DMA), dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), hexamethylphosphoramide (HMPA), N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), tetramethylurea (TMU), trimethylphosphate (TMP), triethylphosphate (TEP) were evaluated as ideal solvents for PVDF [3]. Hansen three-dimensional space was introduced to measure the solubility between solvent and polymer regarding three solubility parameters, dispersion forces, polar forces, and hydrogen bonding. DMA, DMF, NMP, and DMSO are validated as a good solvent for PVDF since the total solubility is less than 5 MPa1/2 [4]. Apparently, PVDF prepared with high dipole moment polar solvent was found to stimulate the β -phase formation, forming longer and better chain orientation which favour its application as piezoelectric, pyroelectric and triboelectric nanogenerator [5,6]. In addition, some efforts have been made by introducing nanofillers such as synthetic ceramics in tetragonal phase and 2D materials into PVDF polymer matrix to improve the dielectric constant and the mechanical properties of PENG [7–9].

Nevertheless, in some study, the synthetic ceramic materials, for instance, barium titanate (BaTiO3) in the cubic phase was deliberately introduced to overcome the undesired structural deformations or microstructure limitation induced by the spin coating method used to obtain β-phase PVDF [10]. This is crucial to improve the sturdiness of mechanical energy harvesting PENG to withstand intensive stress and strain. Besides, 2D materials, specifically with the non-centrosymmetric structure are also reported as a perfect choice for the nanoscale devices as they possessed great mechanical flexibility, piezoelectricity, and ferroelectricity [11–14]. Among those 2D materials, piezoelectricity of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), an element of transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) has been verified theoretically and experimentally [15]. MoS2 has few crystal phases such as a 1-layer hexagonal unit cell (1H), 2-layer hexagonal crystal cell (2H) and 3-layer rhombohedral unit cell (3R) [16]. An interesting behaviour of monolayer MoS2 (1H) only demonstrates piezoelectricity to in-plane d11 direction with piezocoefficient (2.5–4 p.m./V). Nevertheless, this effect is inversely proportional to the number of odd layer of MoS2 and disappear in even layer 2H–MoS2 due to its way of stacking (AB-AB) creates a centrosymmetry structure [17,18]. The non-centrosymmetric materials are essential for mechanical stress to generate the dipole moment. A recent study predicts that 3R–MoS2 has piezoelectric behavior exists in both odd and even layer due to its unique way of vertically stacking (ABC-ABC) removes the centrosymmetry at even layers [19].

The study on distinctive polar solvents and their effects on the piezoelectricity when using merely PVDF thin film nanogenerator has been carried out recently [20]. Introducing two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials into cubic phase BaTiO3 and PVDF composite was found improving the performance of the PENG device [15]. However, the dependency of piezoelectricity behavior by blending 2D MoS2 flakes with cubic phase BaTiO3 and PVDF active layer in DMF and NMP solvents, respectively are yet to be explored. Therefore, a simple all-solution-processed to fabricate PENG devices will be conducted in this work. The device is designed in the structure of silver nanowires (AgNWs)/Nanocomposite/poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) using spin coating and spray coating techniques. The solution process method is a preferable low-cost process yet able to achieve uniform, flexible and nanoscopic scale thin film deposition without the requirement of high vacuum, temperature, expensive and complicated experimental setup [21].

Two PENG devices with the inclusion of MoS2 flakes were fabricated, namely PENG-DMF and PENG-NMP, as the devices were prepared in DMF and NMP solvents, respectively. The PENG devices were fabricated in the structure of AgNWs/Nanocomposite/PEDOT:PSS/PET as shown in Fig. 1 a. The inset shows the top view of the transparent nanocomposite thin film with the grey area as top AgNWs electrode and the dotted line area representing the bottom electrode of PEDOT:PSS. As aforementioned, the nanocomposite was prepared in DMF and NMP solvents, respectively to study the effect of solvents towards the generated output voltage. MoS2 pristine flakes in ethanol/water solution were purchased from Graphene Supermarket. DMF solvent analytical grade and NMP solvent Emplura were purchased from Merck. BaTiO3 powder with an average diameter less than 100 nm, PVDF powder with 1.74 g/mL, and PEDOT:PSS solution were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. AgNWs solution with an average diameter of 40 nm and length of 20–30 μm was supplied by ACS Material.

BaTiO3 and PVDF were used as purchased. 0.2 g BaTiO3 powder was dispersed into 4 ml of DMF and NMP solutions, respectively and sonicated the solutions for 2 h at room temperature to prepare the BaTiO3 solution. 0.2 g PVDF powder was added into 10 ml of DMF and NMP solvents, respectively, stirred for 2 h in a 60 °C water bath to obtain PVDF solution. BaTiO3, MoS2, and PVDF solutions were mixed and ultrasonicated for 10 min in the ratio of 1:1:1 to form the nanocomposite solution. For the fabrication of PENG devices, the PET substrate was treated with oxygen plasma treatment at 30 W for 5 min prior to improve its surface hydrophilicity. Then, PEDOT: PSS was spin-coated at 2000 rpm for 50 s, on top of 2 cm × 2 cm PET substrate to form the 40 nm bottom electrode. A 120 μl of methanol was dropped on the film and dried on the 80 °C hotplate for 15 min to remove the hydrophilic insulator PSS from the film. This step is important to produce a PEDOT:PSS film with enhanced conductivity [22]. Then, the prepared nanocomposite solution was spin-coated at the same speed and duration, to form 130 nm of nanocomposite layer on top of PEDOT:PSS. The nanocomposite layer was left drying on a hotplate at 80 °C for 15 min. Finally, AgNWs were spray coated on top of the nanocomposite layer at 0.1 MPa on a 70 °C hot plate. The same procedures were repeated to fabricate the reference samples, with a nanocomposite ratio of 1:1 for BaTiO3 and PVDF in DMF and NMP solvents, respectively. For ease of clarification, the reference samples without MoS2 flakes were also marked as Reference-DMF and Reference-NMP. Fig. 1b shows the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) cross-sectional image of the fabricated PENG-NMP device.

X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Bruker D8 Advance were used to characterize the crystallinity of nanocomposite thin film. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), SU8230 was used to observe the cross-sectional view of nanocomposite materials and electrode. The absolute transmittance measurements were characterized with PerkinElmer Lambda 900 UV VIS NIR Spectrometer and the performance of nanogenerator was determined using an oscilloscope, Agilent DSO 9404A to obtain the open-circuit voltage when mechanical stress applied on the nanogenerator. A constant 20 N self-assembled tapping machine is used to provide a constant tapping force to the PENG device as shown in Fig. 1c. Fig. 1d shows the picture of the constant tapping force experimental setup. A Teflon stack was installed to the solenoid actuator to deliver an impact area of 1 cm2 at a constant frequency of 5 Hz to the nanogenerator connected to an oscilloscope. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) system (JEOL, JEM-2010) was used to characterize the morphologies, crystallinity, and lattice constant of BaTiO3 and MoS2 nanocomposite thin film.

The atomic structure of the MoS2–BaTiO3 nanocomposites was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and selective area electron diffraction (SAED). Fig. 2 a shows a low-magnification bright-field TEM image of a MoS2 flake decorated with the BaTiO3 nanoparticles transferred on a lacey carbon-coated Cu grid. The large-area MoS2 flake (a few hundred square nanometers) in a form of stacked layers was observed, which are highly electron transparent even in comparison to the lacey carbon support. The most transparent areas are likely to be single a layer, in which the color contrast of the layers indicates the nature of stacking-structures with each other during the sample preparation. Here, the stacked layers were mostly observed at the edges of the MoS2 flakes with an interlayer spacing of 0.62 nm, which is corresponding to the (002) lattice plane (see the inset of Fig. 2a). In Fig. 2b, high-resolution TEM showed that the stacked-layers of the MoS2 consists of lattice spacing of 0.27 nm and 0.16 nm corresponding to the (100) and (110) plane of MoS2, respectively [23,24]. The nature of the MoS2 stacked layers was confirmed by fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern, which two sets of hexagonally arranged diffraction spots with a rotation angle of 20° were observed as in the inset of Fig. 2b. Also, signatures of the Moiré patterns, resulting from possible relative misorientation between each layer were also observed from the Fourier filtered HR-TEM image (see Fig. 2c), suggesting that a MoS2 layer is expected to be stacked randomly with other MoS2 layers. Fig. 2d shows a high-resolution TEM image of BaTiO3 nanoparticles with a lattice spacing of 0.41 nm and 0.41 nm corresponding to the (001) and (010) plane of typical cubic BaTiO3. Most of the BaTiO3 nanoparticles with size ranging from 20 nm to 80 nm, were preferably agglomerated in a form of clusters onto the surface of MoS2 flakes (see the inset of Fig. 2d). Furthermore, SAED characterization was also carried out in this study to further investigate the crystallinity of both the MoS2 flakes and BaTiO3 nanoparticles and the results are shown in Fig. 2e-f. From the analysis of SAED images, the hexagonal lattice structure of the few-layer MoS2 revealed the lattice spacing of ∼0.27 nm and ∼0.16 nm for the (100) and (110) planes, respectively. Meanwhile, for the BaTiO3 nanoparticles, the lattice spacing of ∼0.4186 nm, ∼0.2836 nm and ∼0.2089 nm were also determined from the corresponding (100), (110), and (200) planes, respectively [25].

The electrical performance of a piezoelectric nanogenerator can be evaluated by its output voltage when constant stress is applied on the device. In Fig. 3 a, PENG-DMF generates an output peak voltage of 100 mV while PENG-NMP achieves an output peak voltage as high as 750 mV. This achievement can be correlated to the presence of MoS2 flakes as both reference samples without MoS2 show relatively low output voltage as shown in Fig. 3b. Moreover, PENG-NMP has outperformed as compared to PENG-DMF can also be related to the higher dipole moment of NMP (4.09D) as compared to DMF (3.86D) [6]. This observation could be further manifested in Fig. 3b where MoS2 absence in the device. Under the constant tapping force of 20 N, Reference-DMF shows a peak voltage of 20 mV whereas Reference-NMP demonstrates a twofold increase in peak voltage, which is 40 mV. A higher proportion of β-phase PVDF in the NMP solvent is highly affected by the methods of preparation such as spin coating deposition and drying temperature [26]. By spin-coated the PENG-NMP solution to obtain the thinner films and drying at 80 °C, the β-phase PVDF can be enhanced [6]. The drying temperature used in this work favour to the formation of β-phase PVDF, particularly in NMP due to the higher boiling point of NMP as compared to DMF. The boiling points for both solvents of NMP and DMF are reported to be 202 °C and 153 °C, respectively. The probable reason could be the higher boiling point prolongs the crystallization duration of PVDF owing to the lower evaporation rate of the NMP solvent. Thus, the prolong crystallization period promotes to form thermally stable β-phase PVDF thin film with lower porosity as compared to PVDF in DMF solvent [27,28]. Consequently, β-phase crystallization of PVDF comprises of all-trans (TTTT) planar which can be formed readily when fluorine and hydrogen atoms are aligned in a zigzag manner on the carbon chain backbone. The zigzag alignment of fluorine and hydrogen atoms resulting in high dipole moment [29]. XRD patterns in Fig. 4 shows the evaluated β-phase for PVDF dissolved in both solvents, marked with PVDF-NMP and PVDF-DMF, respectively. Both deconvoluted curves observed β-phase at (010) 20.6° and reflecting α-phase at (020) 18.4°. However, PVDF-NMP shows predominant peak intensity at 20.6° as compared to PVDF-DMF, reflecting that the PVDF-NMP has stronger β-phase crystallinity. Therefore, this observation suggesting that the nanogenerator prepared in the NMP solvent has greater piezoelectric potential.

As aforementioned, in Fig. 3a, the significant improvement in higher voltage output has been obtained with the integration of MoS2 flakes into the devices. The noticeable improved β-phase content is probably a result of electrostatic interaction between the randomly oriented MoS2 flakes and –CH2/-CF2 dipoles of the PVDF matrix. The –CH2 dipoles have interacted with S and –CF2 dipoles have interacted with Mo. In the prepared nanocomposite mixture occasion, it is plausible that PVDF chains are occupied the interstitial position among the layer of MoS2 flakes and vice versa. The occupation of interstitial spaces due to the electrostatic interaction leads to the interaction of MoS2. As a consequence of interaction and intercalation, the nucleation of more electroactive crystalize phases in PVDF along with the formation of piezo-active MoS2 is favoured. MoS2 flakes have large specific surface areas and more importantly, unique crystallographic properties of MoS2 with electron trapping capability, enhance the charge accumulation when tapping force is applied on the PENG [8]. PENG-NMP is found to demonstrate the greatest output voltage under the consistent applied force. Therefore, the PENG-NMP device is preferable for potential practical applications in this study. Fig. 3c shows the magnified electrical behavior output of PENG-NMP. An alternate voltage waveform with a consistent period of 200 ms, corresponding with the frequency of tapping machine is observed. Fig. 3d illustrates the working mechanism of the fabricated nanogenerator device. The zigzag alignment of fluorine and hydrogen atoms in PVDF are well aligned in β-phase due to NMP solvent. When the force, F is applied on top of the device’s surface, the device experienced deformation and resulting high dipole moment in PVDF, generated an electric potential. The generated electric potential can be enhanced by electron trapping that happened in the MoS2 charge trapping site, which yielded the first half positive waveform. When the force is released, the second half negative waveform is generated and the device is returned to its initial state. Remarkably, it is worth to mention that the appearance of unintended noise signals can also be observed in Fig. 3c. There are a few possibilities that cause the appearance of the undesired noise. We have speculated that the noise signals recorded by oscilloscope could be induced by the vibration of the solenoid actuator as this unintended noise was not observed in our earlier reported work [7] when gloved finger tapping was applied to generate an output voltage. For this reason, we could deduce that the noise is indirectly generated by the solenoid actuator that fixed to supporting beam which connected to the nanogenerator’s platform as shown in Fig. 1c. It is believed that the noise signal can be reduced or eliminated by separating the common base of the solenoid actuator and the device. Otherwise, it might be associated with the adhesive forces that act between two different substances, i.e. Teflon and the device. Highly sensitive PENG experienced the undesired noise generated by the solenoid actuator when the force is applied on the PENG device. During the extension and retraction of solenoid actuator, redundant vibration causing a spike in the voltage output of PENG and marked as noise in Fig. 3c. The fabricated PENG-NMP devices generated a higher output voltage, therefore, it is worth to carry out further device characterizations. Fig. 5 shows the devices’ transparency measurements for a blank PET, Reference-NMP and PENG-NMP in the visible range. The transmittance of the blank PET is varying from 83.3% to 87.7%, demonstrates to have the highest transparency amongst the devices. Apparently, Reference-NMP and PENG-NMP also demonstrate highly transparent thin films as both devices have similar transmittance results as the blank PET, varying from 83.76% to 86.01%, and from 83.66% to 84.32%, respectively. PENG-NMP shows slightly lower transparency than Reference-NMP due to the addition of MoS2 flakes. Fig. 6 demonstrates a 5-day endurance test comprising of 5000 cycles for PENG-NMP. The measurements were repeated under the 20 N and 5 Hz constant tapping load for 5 consecutive days. It can be seen that in Fig. 6, the PENG consistently generated a stable voltage source without signification degradation over the period. The PENG-NMP device maintained at least 95% of its 1.5 V throughout the endurance testing cycle.

We investigated the effects of DMF and NMP solvents on the electrical performance of PENG. DMF and NMP, both have different dipole moments of 3.86 D and 4.09 D, respectively. Devices prepared with NMP are found to possess greater open circuit voltage because of its higher dipole moment and boiling points. The higher boiling point of NMP tends to delay the evaporation rate of spin-coated nanocomposite layer during the annealing process. This results in crystallization of predominant β-phase and reduction of porosity of PVDF, forming a thermally stable PVDF that improves PENG performance. Furthermore, MoS2 flakes play an important role in electron trapping and charge accumulation in enhancing the device piezoelectricity. Upon the interaction and intercalation between the MoS2 and PVDF polymer chains, a large amount trapped charges transferred to the electrodes of PENG and amplify the output voltage of the device. The PENG-NMP devices demonstrated to generate an average of 750 mV stable peak voltage without significant degradation for 5 days consecutively and maintained at least 95% of its 1.5 V throughout the stability test. Hence, the fabricated optically transparent and mechanically flexible devices pave the way to realize the future next-generation see through and flexible electronic gadgets especially energy harvesting, sensors, wearable devices, and various optoelectronics touch input gadgets.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported in-part by GUP-2018-082 from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Dana Modal Insan (Human Capital Fund) – MI-2019-005 and Dana Impak Perdana (Prime Impact Fund) – DIP-2018-006 from Research University Fund. This work was also supported in-part by University of Malaya (University Malaya Research Grant Programme (UMRG)) – AFR (Frontier Science) (RP038C-17AFR).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Piezoelectric nanowires in energy harvesting applications
Ann. Mater. Sci. Eng.

Mater. 27 (2017) 1-8. doi:10.1002/adfm.201700702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of indium-tin-oxide free, all-solution-processed flexible nanogenerator device using nanocomposite of barium titanate and graphene quantum dots in polyvinylidene fluoride polymer matrix
